title: Natural finishes: oils and waxes
subtitle: When to use raw linseed, walnut oil, tung oil, danish oil, and beeswax
excerpt: A guide to the penetrating oils and waxes used on hand-made wooden objects: what each does, when each is appropriate, and how they are applied. Covers food-contact pieces, outdoor pieces, and general woodwork finishes.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for wood-finishing oils and their properties. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery for finishing schedules and surface preparation.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content. All finishes are applied to fully seasoned, dry timber for best penetration.
Freshly cut or recently carved wood still high in moisture. Finishes applied to green wood do not penetrate evenly and may blister as the wood continues to dry.

content.text: Penetrating oil and wax finishes soak into the wood surface rather than forming a film on top. They feed the wood fibres, bring out the natural colour and figure, and provide varying degrees of protection against moisture and handling. Unlike lacquer or varnish, they do not seal the surface or hide the texture of the wood.

Raw linseed oil is the standard finish for food-contact wooden objects: spoons, bowls, chopping boards, and butter knives. It is non-toxic when fully cured, penetrates deeply, and brings out the colour of pale woods well. Its drawback is drying time: raw linseed takes two to four weeks to cure fully at room temperature. Apply a thin coat with a cloth, allow twenty-four hours, then apply a second coat. Repeat until the surface no longer absorbs oil quickly.

Walnut oil is a food-safe drying oil that cures faster than raw linseed and produces a similar warm finish. It is suitable for spoons, salad bowls, and any piece that contacts food. Its colour is slightly warmer than raw linseed on pale species. Note that walnut oil is not suitable for people with tree-nut allergies; if the piece is a gift, raw linseed is safer.

Pure tung oil is food-safe when cured, highly water-resistant, and more flexible than linseed when dry. It is a good choice for pieces that will be used outdoors or washed regularly. It cures to a slightly darker, more amber finish than linseed. It must be applied in very thin coats; thick coats wrinkle as they dry.

Boiled linseed oil (BLO) contains metallic driers that speed curing to twenty-four to forty-eight hours. It is not food-safe. Use it on garden tools, handles, and seasoned-wood projects that do not contact food. It is the standard finish for tool handles; it seals the grain and prevents moisture uptake during outdoor use.

Danish oil is a blended penetrating finish (typically linseed or tung oil with driers and a small amount of varnish) that cures in four to six hours and produces a slightly harder surface than plain oil. Not food-safe. It is the standard choice for furniture, shelves, frames, and boxes. Apply with a cloth in thin coats, allowing each coat to cure before the next.

Beeswax is applied as the final coat over a cured oil finish to add a low sheen and a smooth feel. It does not penetrate deeply on its own and does not provide sufficient protection used alone on bare wood. Applied over danish oil or boiled linseed, it buffs to a soft matte sheen and fills the surface slightly. Re-apply once or twice a year on frequently handled objects. Board butter (a blend of beeswax and a food-safe oil) is a pre-mixed version that can be used directly on food-contact pieces.
or partially dry wood do not penetrate evenly and will blister or peel as the wood finishes drying. Sand to 400 grit before the first coat; a smooth surface takes oil more evenly than a coarser one.
